coredata.dc:title: Alkali-assisted synthesis of gourd flesh derived tricolor F/N co-doped carbon dots for efficient degradation of organic dyes
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Congling
Xu, Jun
Li, Huizhi
Zhao, Weilin
coredata.dc:description: A novel tricolor F/N co-doped CDs was synthesized by alkali-assisted control with gourd flesh as a precursor, targeting at the catalytic degradation of organic dyes with extremely high catalysis rate. The synthesized nanomaterials were characterized by X-ray diffraction, Transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy techniques, indicating that the tricolor F/N-CDs have good dispersion and abundant surface defects which were gradually increasing from F/N-G-CDs, F/N-Y-CDs to F/N-O-CDs. Through the UV–visible and fluorescence spectra, the tricolor F/N-CDs all exhibited good fluorescence performance and the fluorescence lifetime was nano-scale level. The tricolor F/N-CDs displayed excellent photocatalytic activity which was evaluated by the organic dyes methylene blue (MB) and congo red (CR) as models. The important thing is that under sunlight irradiation, the degradation efficiency of using tricolor F/N-CDs for MB reaches ~100% and the degradation efficiency for CR dye reaches ~98% in a pole short time. With the red shift of the fluorescent tricolor F/N-CDs, the degradation time gradually decreased and the degradation rate gradually increased. The results explained that the prepared tricolor F/N-CDs had excellent application prospects in the natural degradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gourd flesh
Tricolor F/N-CDs
Alkali-assisted synthesis
Catalytic degradation
Degradation rate

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of F/N-G-CDs, F/N-Y-CDs and F/N-O-CDs.
TEM images of (a) F/N-G-CDs, (b) F/N-Y-CDs, (c) F/N-O-CDs. Diameter distributions (Curves: Gauss fit) of (d) F/N-G-CDs, (e) F/N-Y-CDs, (f) F/N-O-CDs.
FT-IR spectra of F/N-G-CDs, F/N-Y-CDs and F/N-O-CDs.

High resolution (a) Full‐survey, (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s, (d) N 1s and (e) Na 1s XPS spectra of F/N-G-CDs, High resolution (f) Full‐survey, (g) C 1s, (h) O 1s, (i) N 1s XPS and (j) Na 1s spectra of F/N-Y-CDs, High resolution (k) Full‐survey, (l) C 1s, (m) O 1s, (n) N 1s and (o) Na 1s XPS spectra of F/N-O-CDs.

(a) UV–visble absorption spectra (inset: photographs under ambient light and 365 nm UV light) and (b) excitation spectra of F/N-CDs, (c–e) fluorescence emission spectra of F/N-G-CDs, F/N-Y-CDs and F/N-O-CDs at different excitation wavelengths, (f) Decay curves of the F/N-CDs.

(a) Catalytic degradation and (b) pseudo-first-order kinetics rates of tricolor F/N-CDs catalysts for degradation of MB dye. (c) Effect of tricolor F/N-CDs catalysts on degradation of CR dye and (d) the corresponding pseudo-first-order linear fit data. (e) Digital photograph of MB and CR dye catalytic degradation.

Catalytic degradation of (a) MB dye and (b) CR dye over F/N-G-CDs, F/N-Y-CDs and F/N-O-CDs. (f) Illustration showing the possible degradation mechanism; (g) Plausible mechanism depicted by reactions involved in the degradation.
The element content of F/N-G-CDs, F/N-Y-CDs and F/N-O-CDs.
Comparison of the optical properties of CDs derived from different carbon source.

head.ce:title: Alkali-assisted synthesis of gourd flesh derived tricolor F/N co-doped carbon dots for efficient degradation of organic dyes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Preparation and Measurement of Building Materials, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Preparation and Measurement of Building Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Preparation and Measurement of Building Materials, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel tricolor F/N co-doped CDs was synthesized by alkali-assisted control with gourd flesh as a precursor, targeting at the catalytic degradation of organic dyes with extremely high catalysis rate. The synthesized nanomaterials were characterized by X-ray diffraction, Transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy techniques, indicating that the tricolor F/N-CDs have good dispersion and abundant surface defects which were gradually increasing from F/N-G-CDs, F/N-Y-CDs to F/N-O-CDs. Through the UV–visible and fluorescence spectra, the tricolor F/N-CDs all exhibited good fluorescence performance and the fluorescence lifetime was nano-scale level. The tricolor F/N-CDs displayed excellent photocatalytic activity which was evaluated by the organic dyes methylene blue (MB) and congo red (CR) as models. The important thing is that under sunlight irradiation, the degradation efficiency of using tricolor F/N-CDs for MB reaches ~100% and the degradation efficiency for CR dye reaches ~98% in a pole short time. With the red shift of the fluorescent tricolor F/N-CDs, the degradation time gradually decreased and the degradation rate gradually increased. The results explained that the prepared tricolor F/N-CDs had excellent application prospects in the natural degradation.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) generally refer to zero-dimensional nanoclusters with spherical shape and diameter of less than 10 nm, which are composed of carbon as the skeleton [1,2]. The surface is composed of a variety of organic functional groups (C=C, N-H, O=C-OH and O-H, etc.) [3]. The preparation methods of CDs can be divided into top-down and bottom-up. The bottom-up method is through electrochemical oxidation [4], hydrothermal/solvothermal [5,6] and microwave-assisted heating [7] to make various organic small molecules and polymer monomers dehydrate, carbonize at high temperature, cross-link to form a stable carbonaceous core, or through further surface functionalization or passivation to finally obtain fluorescent CDs [8,9]. What's more, compared with the top-down method, the bottom-up method is more simple in which the hydrothermal method is widely applied as a result of its easy process and control, and environmentally friendly products. After irradiating with ultraviolet light, the charges of CDs are separated and then quickly recombined, thus showing the photoluminescence characteristics, which mainly depends on its surface state [10]. Therefore, surface functionalization [11] and heteroatom (O, N, P, S, F) doping [12] are effective methods to adjust the optical properties of CDs.

Presently, carbon-containing natural substances and chemicals can be employed as precursor materials to fabricate carbon dots. Among them, the most economical and environmentally friendly raw material is organic biomass [13,14]. According to reports, CDs were synthesized from precursors such as citrus grandis [15], plant extract [16], sugarcane bagasse [17], bran [18], palm [19], agaricus bisporus [20], eggshell membrane [21] and so on. Gourd belongs to Cucurbitaceae family, in which gourd flesh has high medicinal value in diuresis and swelling. The major chemical constituents present in the gourd flesh are vitamins, carbohydrates, protein, cellulose, flavonoids, pectin, choline, as well as trace element with certain biological activity including F element. As a precursor, gourd flesh not only provides a carbon source, but also can realize self-doping of F element and the CDs prepared therefrom have considerable activity.

For the applicative perspectives, CDs are currently applied in photocatalysis [22] in addition to being utilized in bioimaging [23], phototherapy [24] and sensing [25] fields. With the development of industry, the water pollution caused by organic contaminants, such as dye effluent, is aggravating [26]. The existing treatment technology of dye effluent will also produce secondary pollution and high cost, which further restrict its wide application. Therefore, it is imperative to develop novel technologies to eliminate harmful organic pollutants. Photocatalytic oxidation has become one of the most promising chemical technologies for dye effluent due to its mild reaction conditions and high efficiency. Among dye effluent discharges, Methylene blue (MB) as one of the widely used textile dye [27,28] has an organic structure of phenothiazine salt, very well known to cause irritation to the gastrointestinal tract and skin under continuous exposure. Congo red (CR) is a typical benzidine anionic azo dye, based on aromatic hydrocarbons, containing two polar groups of amino (-NH2) sodium sulfonate (-SO3Na) and two azo groups (N=N-). However, as an azo dye, CR has great harm to the ecological environment and human health. Azo dyes are dyes that contain –N=N- in their molecular structure and are a synthetic dye that is widely used in paper, leather, plastic and textile industries. It can be decomposed into more than 20 kinds of carcinogenic aromatic amines under certain conditions, and azo dyes will be decomposed into aromatic amines after entering the human body, which can cause cancer in tissues and organs such as ureter, renal pelvis and bladder. Aromatic amines have stable chemical properties, are not easily degraded in the natural environment, and can be enriched in the human body through the food chain, which is very harmful. Therefore, it is of great significance to remove CR pollution in industrial waste effluents.

Herein, we proposed a facile approach based on alkali-assisted treatment to synthesis tricolor F/N-CDs, and studied their catalytic application for the efficient removal of MB and CR dyes. Tricolor F/N-CDs were fabricated using gourd flesh with high medicinal value as a precursor to provide C and F, and urea as a N source. Controlling the amount of NaOH added during the preparation of F/N-CDs was varied to produce different optical properties. The results showed that green-emitting F/N-CDs, yellow-emitting CDs and orange-emitting CDs possessed significant catalytic degradation performance of organic dyes.

Gourd flesh came from sawn yellow gourd that was wild and mature. The chemical NaOH was purchased from Aladdin Reagent Network. Urea was purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Anhydrous ethanol was purchased from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). The organic dyes methylene blue (MB) and congo red (CR) were obtained from Bomei Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Hefei, China). All reagents are analytical grade and can be used without further purification. The 0.2 μm microporous filtration membrane was purchased from Tianjin Jinteng Experimental Equipment Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). The experimental water was deionized water.

Tricolor F/N-CDs were succeeded in synthesizing via one-step hydrothermal treatment from naturally gourd flesh. The precursor (gourd flesh) was beaten into fine powder by a universal crusher. Specifically, three beakers were added to 0.3 g gourd crumb powder, 0.1 g urea and 20 ml absolute ethanol solution, followed by adding 0.1 g, 0.2 g, and 0.3 g solid NaOH, respectively. The resulting solution was kept stirring at 30 °C for 15 min to make evenly mixed, poured into a reactor (lined with 50 ml PTFE tank), then heated in a furnace at 200 °C for 8 h. The F/N-CDs solution obtained was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to separate solid and liquid. Microporous membrane was applied to further dislodge solid particles from the collected supernatant. Then, the gathered filtrate was put into a dialysis bag (1000 Da MWCO) and dialyzed in a magnetic stirrer for 24 h to remove small particles for further purification. Finally, three F/N-CDs solutions were collected, and which excited by a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp behaved green (G), yellow (Y) and orange (O)-emitting, referred to as F/N-X-CD (X = G, Y, O). The synthesis scheme of F/N-G-CDs, F/N-Y-CDs and F/N-O-CDs is exhibited in Fig. 1 .

The crystalline properties were recorded by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Ultima IV) equipped with Cu Kα (λ = 1.5418 Å) radiation in the 2θ range of 10–80° at a scan rate of 4°/min. The morphological images and size distribution of tricolor F/N-CDs were analyzed by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20) under an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The functional groups of F/N-CDs was characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR, Thermo Nicolet 380) in the region from 4000 to 400 cm−1. An X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measured by a Thermo ESCALAB XI+ was used to characterize the elemental presence and composition of the F/N-CDs. The UV–visible absorption spectra were undertaken by using a Metash UV-5800 spectrophotometer in the scanning range of 300–700 nm. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra and the decay curves were obtained on Shimadzu RF-6500PC fluorescence spectrometer.

The XRD spectra of F/N-G-CDs, F/N-Y-CDs and F/N-O-CDs are displayed in Fig. 2 . As can be seen, the XRD patterns of F/N-CDs show a wide and intense peak at around 2θ = 30.2°, which is attributed to the amorphous nature of tricolor F/N-CDs [29]. The atomic distance (d) between the two crystal planes is 0.296 nm calculated from the Bragg equation 2d sinθ = λ. The generation of the wide diffraction peak can be attributed to the small particle size, low crystallinity, and high dispersion of the F/N-CDs prepared [30].

In Fig. 3 , TEM is utilized to characterize the morphology of tricolor F/N-CDs due to its intuitiveness. Fig. 3 (ãc) represents the TEM results that tricolor F/N-CDs were uniformly monodispersed and nearly spherical in shape. Fig. 3 (d ~ f) reveals the histogram size distribution which is well agreed with Gaussian fitting of the corresponding tricolor F/N-CDs particles. The F/N-G-CDs, F/N-Y-CDs and F/N-O-CDs had similar average particle sizes at ~6.07 nm, ~4.58 nm and ~5.09 nm,respectively.

FT-IR spectroscopy is measured for determining the surface structure and functional groups due to its extremely high structural sensitivity. The FT-IR spectra of gourd flesh and tricolor F/N-CDs are demonstrated in Fig. 4 . The broad peak located at about 3460 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of O-H/N-H groups in multi-molecular association form [31]. The weak peak at about 2970 cm−1 corresponds to the anti-symmetric stretching vibration of –CH3. The absorption band at 1590 cm−1 can be assigned to the asymmetric stretching vibration of carboxylate C=O [32]. The peaks at 1120 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of C-F group. Compared with the gourd flesh, two sharp peaks are added at 1460 cm−1 and 866 cm−1, which are attributed to the stretching vibration of C-O/C-N [33] and the out-of-plane bending vibration of N-H functional group respectively. With the increase of NaOH, the vibration intensity of the two new peaks increases indicating that the C-O/C-N and N-H functional groups gradually increase.
Fig. 4FT-IR spectra of F/N-G-CDs, F/N-Y-CDs and F/N-O-CDs.

The optical characteristics of the tricolor F/N-CDs are the most valuable properties for basic research, including ultraviolet visible absorption, photoluminescence, excitation wavelength dependence and so on.

In Fig. 6 (a), the UV–visble absorption spectra indicate that tricolor F/N-CDs have obvious light absorption characteristics extending from the UV to visble-light region, with an absorption edge at around 500 nm. Distinct absorption peaks at 335 nm of the prepared tricolor F/N-CDs correspond to the π-π* transition of the oxygen-containing group (C=O) on the surface of the carbon nucleus [37–40]. The characteristic absorption peak after 342 nm belongs to n-π* transition, and with the red shift of the fluorescence emission of F/N-CDs, the plateau area of peak gradually increases. The tricolor F/N-CDs exhibit almost the same adsorption, but an enhanced absorption intensity in the UV-light region is observed with the red shift of the fluorescence emission which indicated that the contents of the oxygen-containing functional groups and crystalline graphene quantum gradually increased [41]. The insets are the photos of tricolor F/N-CDs under ambient light and 365 nm UV light. Corresponding to 0.1 g, 0.2 g and 0.3 g NaOH, the tricolor F/N-CDs change from light brown to dark brown under ambient light, whist emerge green, yellow, and orange fluorescence emission under 365 nm UV light, respectively.

Photoluminescence spectra of tricolor F/N-CDs are showed in Fig. 6 (b ~ e). The optimum excitation wavelengths of F/N-G-CDs, F/N-Y-CDs and F/N-O-CDs are respectively 470 nm, 500 nm and 530 nm in Fig. 6 (b). Fig. 6(c–e) are the fluorescence emission spectra of F/N-G-CDs, F/N-Y-CDs and F/N-O-CDs. The corresponding maximum emission wavelengths at the optimal excitation wavelength are 528 nm, 582 nm and 598 nm. The emission peaks of tricolor F/N-CDs gradually red-shift with the increase of excitation wavelength, which confirms the excitation wavelength dependence, and abundant energy levels exist on the surface of tricolor F/N-CDs [42–44]. Table 2 shows the comparison of the optical properties of synthesized CDs derived from various plant carbon sources. The CDs synthesized using gourd flesh have tunable emission wavelength and a wider emission wavelength range compared to the reported works [19,20,45–48].

Decay curves (Fig. 6 (f)) evaluate the fluorescence lifetime of tricolor F/N-CDs which is the characteristic period remaining excited before returning to the ground state. The fluorescence lifetimes are 2.09 ns (F/N-G-CDs), 4.84 ns (F/N-Y-CDs) and 6.43 ns (F/N-O-CDs) obtained through two-exponential fitting. The nano-scale fluorescence lifetimes of tricolor F/N-CDs indicates that the carbon cores have abundant surface defects, which is conducive to the radiation recombination of the tricolor F/N-CDs to produce photoluminescence.

The catalytic properties of tricolor F/N-CDs catalysts are evaluated by degrading the toxic MB and CR dyes when exposed to dark and the sunlight irradiation [49] (Fig. 7 ). The dye concentration is monitored by measuring the maximum absorbance with a Metash UV-5800 spectrophotometer.

For the degradation of MB dyes, 10 mL of F/N-G-CDs, F/N-Y-CDs or F/N-G-CDs is added in 30 mL 30 mg/L MB aqueous solution, respectively. The three samples obtained are stirred in the dark for 20 min to establish the adsorption-desorption equilibrium [50] between the model pollutant and F/N-CDs catalysts. Subsequently, the samples are exposed to sunlight for 90 min, and 3 ml is drawn every 10 min for testing and the degradation efficiency is calculated, as shown in Fig. 7 (a). The blank MB solution emerges no clear degradation after 90 min irradiation, manifesting that MB has strong photo-stability, so the choice of MB as catalytic model has research significance. In order to be more precise, each experiment was repeated 3 times to guarantee the reproducibility of the catalytic performance. There was no obvious change in the degradation efficiency, demonstrating that tricolor F/N-CDs had good stability as catalysts. The degradation rate of MB dye by tricolor F/N-CDs is ~100% under 60 min, 70 min and 90 min of sunlight irradiation, respectively. Furthermore, the degradation data in Fig. 7 (a) is fitted according to the pseudo first-order kinetic equation ln(C0/C) = kt, resulting in Fig. 7 (b). In the pseudo-first-order kinetic model, t is the, C0 and C are the concentration of dye model at reaction time 0 and t, respectively, and k is the rate constant. R2 is the fitting degree, and the closer to 1, the better the fitting. The ln(C0/C) values of tricolor F/N-CDs catalysts are in good linear with respect to the reaction time. The rate constant k of MB dye by tricolor F/N-CDs is in the order: F/N-O-CDs (0.0678) > F/N-Y-CDs (0.0566) > F/N-O-CDs (0.0432).

For the degradation of CR dyes, 10 mL tricolor F/N-CDs were stirred with 30 mL 30 mg/L CR dye in the dark environment for 5 min, followed by irradiating under sunlight for 20 min. During the degradation reaction, ~3 mL of the solution was sampled at 5 min intervals. Fig. 7(c and d) showed the influence of F/N-G-CDs, F/N-Y-CDs and F/N-O-CDs on degradation of 30 mg/L CR solution. In an initial 5 min of dark environment experiments, it was observed that ~1% of CR is adsorbed on the tricolor F/N-CDs, establishing an adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Subsequently, under 10 min, 15 min and 20 min of sunlight irradiation, ~98% degradation of CR dye was completed by tricolor F/N-CDs, respectively. The self-degradation of CR under 90-min sunlight was negligible (only 1%). The rate constant k of CR over CDs samples is in the order: F/N-O-CDs (0.4095) > F/N-Y-CDs (0.2706) > F/N-O-CDs (0.2059).

In order to further explore the active species of dyes degradation with tricolor F/N-CDs, using a variety of scavengers to carry out free radical capture experiments have been performed [51]. In Fig. 8 (a,b), the effect of using different types of scavengers [52] on the catalytic degradation of MB and CR dyes by tricolor F/N-CDs are displayed, along with the group without trapping agents as a control. When electrons and holes are captured by the Na2-EDTA complex, the degradation rate decreases. Other species used as traps [53], such as benzoquinone (BQ) for superoxide (·O2 −) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) for hydroxyl (·OH) are not as effective as Na2-EDTA (e - and h+). Comparing the effects of each free radical on the photocatalytic degradation efficiency, electrons and holes seem to be the main reason for the catalytic activity. Based on the trapping experiment, the possible mechanism diagram and related reactions of the tricolor F/N-CDs for catalytic degradation of dyes are shown in Fig. 8 (c,d). When photons with high-voltage energy radiate F/N-CDs, electrons transition from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), and holes are generated in the valence band (Eq. (1)) [54]. The charge carrier (electrons or holes) have strong reducing and oxidizing capabilities. The electrons react with O2 to form superoxide radical O2 − (Eq (2)), and the holes oxidize water to form hydroxyl radicals ·OH (Eq (3)) [55]. Therefore, superoxide radicals or hydroxyl radicals adsorbed on the surface of F/N-CDs act as reactive oxygen species (ROS) to interact with dye molecules and photodegrade them into smaller molecules/fragments (Eq.(4) &(5)).

In the current research, the wasted gourd flesh as C and F sources and urea as N source were applied to prepare tricolor F/N co-doped carbon dots (F/N-CDs) for the catalytic degradation of MB and CR dyes. A facile hydrothermal method was used to prepare tricolor F/N-CDs (green, yellow and orange emitting) using different concentrations of NaOH with varying F and N contents. The structure and morphological chemical composition of tricolor F/N-CDs were characterized through a variety of analytical techniques, such as XRD, TEM, FT-IR and XPS. In addition, the optical properties of the synthesized tricolor F/N-CDs were characterized by UV–visible spectra and fluorescence spectra. Furthermore, the catalytic activity of tricolor F/N-CDs to degrade environmental pollutants MB and CR was evaluated under sunlight radiation, and the degradation rates reached ~100% and ~98% respectively in a short time. With the red shift of fluorescence F/N-CDs, the degradation rate constants of MB (0.0432 min−1, 0.0566 min−1, 0.0678 min−1) and CR dyes (0.2059 min−1, 0.2706 min−1, 0.4095 min−1) gradually increased. The excellent photocatalytic performance of the tricolor F/N-CDs system obtained from trapping experiment was attributed to the increased conversion of ·O2 − and the effective separation of electron-hole pairs under sunlight irradiation. The results showed that the novel synthesized catalysts were promising in the degradation of organic dyes.

Congling Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jun Xu: Validation, Investigation. Huizhi Li: Supervision, Project administration. Weilin Zhao: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant No. 51802114) and the Shandong key research and development plan (No. 2017GGX20127).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Common origin of green luminescence in carbon nanodots and graphene quantum dots
ACS Nano
